title: NOC 82021 – Contractors and supervisors, oil and gas drilling and services | GTR Immigration
description: Contractors and supervisors under NOC 82021 are the technical leaders of Canada's world-class energy sector. As a TEER 2 occupation, these professionals coordinate the complex operations of drilling for oil and gas, as well as the essential well-servicing activities that maintain production. In 2026, as Canada integrates "Clean-Tech" and carbon-capture technology into traditional energy extraction, these supervisors are critical for managing high-stakes environments where safety and precision are paramount. For immigration, this role is a premier candidate for the Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP) and the Alberta Opportunity Stream.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level2Major Group82 – Supervisors in natural resources, agriculture and related productionSub-Major Group820 – Supervisors in natural resources, agriculture and related productionMinor Group8202 – Contractors and supervisors, mining, oil and gasNOC Code82021

duties: Supervise, coordinate, and schedule the activities of workers who drill for oil and gas or provide essential well services.
Establish methods to meet work schedules and coordinate activities with other departments or sub-contractors.
Requisition materials and supplies and manage the maintenance of multi-million dollar drilling equipment.
Resolve work problems and recommend measures to improve productivity and well-site safety.
Train workers in job duties, advanced safety procedures, and the operation of new drilling technology.
Ensure strict adherence to provincial energy regulations and environmental protection protocols.
Prepare detailed production, safety, and inventory reports for management and regulatory bodies.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is required.
Work Experience: Several years of experience in the occupations supervised (e.g., as a driller or service rig operator) are mandatory.
Certifications: Specialized certificates such as Blowout Prevention (BOP), H2S Alive, and First Aid are mandatory.
Leadership: Strong project management skills and the ability to manage diverse crews in remote, high-pressure environments are essential.
A college or university program in petroleum technology or engineering is increasingly preferred for senior "Company Man" or Rig Manager roles.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, experienced Rig Managers in Alberta often earn annual salaries exceeding $160,000, inclusive of field premiums.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. According to the Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS), the sector is experiencing a "Technological Re-skilling" phase. While the number of active rigs fluctuates, the complexity of each well site is increasing, requiring more supervisors per project. Retirements will remain the primary driver of new openings through 2028.
labour_market_demand: The demand for oil and gas supervisors is Very Good, particularly in the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin. In 2026, Alberta (Fort McMurray/Grand Prairie) and Saskatchewan (Lloydminster/Estevan) are seeing a massive surge in demand due to the expansion of natural gas exports and lithium extraction from oilfield brines. With a significant portion of the workforce reaching retirement age, Canadian energy firms are actively seeking international talent with robust field experience.
Energy Transition Leadership: Lead operations in a country transitioning toward "Net-Zero" production, gaining experience in carbon sequestration and hydrogen-ready infrastructure.
Premium Compensation: This is one of the highest-paying supervisory roles in Canada, often including remote living allowances and high-performance bonuses.
Global Innovation: Work with the world's most advanced drilling technology, including automated "Walking Rigs" and real-time subsurface data analytics.
PR Strategic Pathway: High priority for Express Entry Category-Based Selection (Trades) and Alberta’s Tourism and Hospitality/Energy Streams.
pathways: Express Entry (Category-Based Selection): Targeted for candidates with experience in "Trade" occupations.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: A high-priority pathway for supervisors currently working in Alberta’s energy sector.
Saskatchewan INP (Occupations In-Demand): Frequently prioritizes supervisors in the oil, gas, and mining sectors.
Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP): A reliable route for supervisors with the required work experience and a job offer.
